attempts.raw_response:
<reasoning>
Proceed by induction on the first sortedness proof `sxs`. The empty-left and singleton-left cases are easy by simplification/case split on `ys`. In the `cons` case, split on `ys`; if `ys` is empty the merge is just the left list. If `ys = [y]`, then split on `a < y`; in the true branch the result is exactly `a :: y :: b :: xs` and sortedness follows from `Sorted.cons` using transitivity from `a < y` and `¬ b < a`; in the false branch the result is `y :: a :: b :: xs` and sortedness follows similarly using `¬ a < y`. If `ys = y :: z :: ys'`, split on `a < y`; in the true branch use the IH on `Sorted (b :: xs)` and `Sorted (y :: z :: ys')`, then prepend `a` and prove `¬ head(merge ...) < a` by another split on `b < y`. In the false branch, first derive `Sorted (merge (a :: b :: xs) (z :: ys'))` by applying the main theorem recursively to the same left list and the tail-sortedness of `ys` (obtained from `lemma_hint_3`), then prepend `y` and prove `¬ head(...) < y` by splitting on `a < z`.
</reasoning>

attempts.raw_response:
<reasoning>
Proceed by induction on the first sortedness proof `sxs`. The empty-left and singleton-left cases are easy by simplification/case split on `ys`. In the `cons` case, split on `ys`; if `ys` is empty the merge is just the left list. If `ys = [y]`, then split on `a < y`; in the true branch the result is exactly `a :: y :: b :: xs` and sortedness follows from `Sorted.cons` using transitivity from `a < y` and `¬ b < a`; in the false branch the result is `y :: a :: b :: xs` and sortedness follows similarly using `¬ a < y`. If `ys = y :: z :: ys'`, split on `a < y`; in the true branch use the IH on `Sorted (b :: xs)` and `Sorted (y :: z :: ys')`, then prepend `a` and prove `¬ head(merge ...) < a` by another split on `b < y`. In the false branch, first derive `Sorted (merge (a :: b :: xs) (z :: ys'))` by applying the main theorem recursively to the same left list and the tail-sortedness of `ys` (obtained from `lemma_hint_3`), then prepend `y` and prove `¬ head(...) < y` by splitting on `a < z`.
</reasoning>
